advantages: Explains why adding features can hurt validation metrics even when columns are cheap to compute — guides disciplined feature engineering.
Unifies symptoms (k-NN collapse, unstable clustering, need for huge n) under sparsity, distance concentration, and sample complexity.
Justifies dimensionality reduction and regularization before algorithm tuning — saves iteration on doomed high-d pipelines.
Clarifies when local nonparametric methods are appropriate (low-to-moderate d, ample n) versus when parametric or tree models are safer.
Connects Bellman's dynamic-programming grid explosion to modern ML feature-space geometry — same exponential scaling intuition.
Helps set realistic data-collection targets: exponential growth in required n with d is a planning constraint, not a software bug.
disadvantages: Can be overstated as 'high d is always bad' — sparse high-d data (e.g. bag-of-words) and linear separability can still work with the right model and metric.
Distance concentration severity depends on distribution and feature correlation; i.i.d. uniform intuition is not universal for structured embeddings.
Does not prescribe which reduction method to use — PCA, LDA, autoencoders, and feature selection require separate topic-level decisions.
Sample-complexity bounds are often pessimistic; low intrinsic dimensionality (manifold structure) can make learning feasible despite large ambient d.
Focusing on d alone ignores label noise, class imbalance, and model mismatch — the curse is one factor among several in poor generalization.
Mitigations (more data, reduction) have cost: PCA can discard rare but predictive directions if variance-based cuts are applied blindly.
